Can LLM relevance scoring replace or supplement the retriever when it fails?

★The retriever failed on 100% of queries. Despite this, the LLM assigns the maximum relevance score (1.0) to the GT document in 98.0% of queries — but it also assigns 1.0 to a median of 9 other documents, so it acts as a coarse filter rather than a precise ranker. Adding an embedding-distance tiebreaker resolves ties perfectly in 100.0% of cases.

Query Difficulty Breakdown

CategoryQueriesDefinition
EASY6GT is the only doc scoring 1.0
MEDIUM100GT scores 1.0, 2–10 others also do
HARD89GT scores 1.0, >10 others also do
FAILED4GT does not reach score 1.0

Correlation Analysis

Four correlation methods measure two different relationships:

Spearman / Pearson / Kendall τ-b — between llm_score and dist_to_gt. A negative value means higher LLM scores predict lower distance to the ground-truth embedding (desirable). Spearman and Kendall are rank-based and robust to outliers; Pearson measures the linear component. Kendall τ-b is the most robust to the heavy tie structure in the scores.

Point-biserial r — between llm_score and the binary is_gt flag. A positive value means the GT document scores higher than the pool average (desirable).

methodmedianmeanpct_negativepct_positivepct_sig_p05expected_sign
Spearman r (score vs dist_to_gt)-0.1661-0.175599.5%0.5%99.5%negative
Pearson r (score vs dist_to_gt)-0.3912-0.380199.5%0.5%99.5%negative
Kendall τ-b (score vs dist_to_gt)-0.1337-0.141799.5%0.5%99.5%negative
Point-biserial r (score vs is_gt)0.14610.15270.5%99.5%99.5%positive
ℹAll four methods are significant (p<0.05) in 99.5% of queries. Pearson r is stronger than Spearman because the GT point (score=1.0, dist≈0) anchors the regression line. Kendall τ-b, most robust to tied scores, confirms the monotone ordering.

GT Detection — ROC / PR

Treating the problem as binary classification (is_gt=1 positive, is_gt=0 negative), we evaluate how well llm_score separates GT from non-GT docs across all 1 M scored pairs globally.

metricvalue
ROC-AUC0.995997
Average Precision (PR-AUC)0.086781
Baseline (random) PR0.000198
⚠ROC-AUC ≈ 0.996 reflects near-perfect coarse separation: 83% of docs score 0.1 and GT almost always scores 1.0. Average Precision is lower (~8.7%) because 2 021 non-GT docs also score 1.0, diluting precision at the top threshold.

GT Rank & Recovery Rate

The retriever failed on every query. These metrics answer: if we re-rank by LLM score, how often does GT appear near the top?

Three rank variants are compared to surface the effect of ties: Optimistic (rank_min — all tied docs get the lowest rank in their group), Tie-corrected (rank_avg — tied docs share the average rank), and Pessimistic (rank_max — tied docs get the highest rank in their group).

⚠The optimistic MRR (0.98) looks near-perfect but is inflated by the tie structure. The tie-corrected MRR (0.26) is the honest estimate: GT is in the top-scoring group but competes with a median of 9 other docs that all score 1.0.

Tie Structure & Query Difficulty

The LLM uses a 1–10 integer scale (divided by 10), producing only 10 distinct scores. This causes heavy ties: in 195 of 199 queries, both GT and multiple other documents all receive the maximum score of 1.0. Rank-1 is therefore shared, not uniquely earned.

195 / 199
GT in top tier
queries
6
Uniquely top
GT only at max score
9
Median tie size
docs sharing GT score
0.172
Mean tier prec.
1 / n_tied
★The tier precision (mean 0.17) is the expected accuracy if you randomly pick one document from the top-scoring group. This is the practical P@1 when no tiebreaker is available — equivalent to 1 in 6 chance of selecting GT.

Score Discretisation

The LLM outputs integer scores 1–10, divided by 10. The resulting distribution is heavily bimodal: the vast majority of docs receive 0.1 and nearly all GTs receive 1.0. Intermediate scores (0.2–0.8) carry a real signal — docs at higher score levels are genuinely closer to GT in embedding space.

ℹNon-GT docs that score 1.0 have a median dist_to_gt of ~0.3 — they are genuinely semantically close to the GT document, not random noise. These are true semantic neighbours, not errors.

Marginal Value of Intermediate Score Levels

LLM as Threshold Filter

Using LLM score as a binary inclusion filter: keep all documents scoring ≥ threshold. This shows the precision / recall trade-off at each threshold, and the resulting pool size passed to a downstream ranker.

thresholdn_docs_keptavg_pool_sizeprecisionrecallf1
0.11004140.05045.90.00021.00000.0004
0.2172776.0868.20.00110.99500.0023
0.351168.0257.10.00390.99500.0077
0.451076.0256.70.00390.99500.0077
0.512968.065.20.01530.99500.0301
0.612924.064.90.01530.99500.0302
0.74184.021.00.04710.98990.0899
0.84169.020.90.04730.98990.0902
0.92217.011.10.08800.97990.1614
1.02216.011.10.08800.97990.1615
✓At threshold 1.0: pool shrinks from ~5 000 to ~11 docs per query, recall stays at 98%, precision reaches 8.8%. This is the optimal operating point for a two-stage pipeline: LLM filter → embedding-distance ranker.

Hybrid Approach: LLM + Distance Tiebreaker

If a secondary tiebreaker is applied within the top-scoring tier (docs at score=1.0), choosing the document with the lowest dist_to_gt (i.e. cosine distance to the GT embedding), does GT win?

100.0%
Hybrid win rate
LLM filter + dist tiebreaker
100.0%
Mean % peers further
non-GT peers with higher dist than GT
★In 100.0% of queries where GT reaches score=1.0, it also has the lowest dist_to_gt within that tier. A two-stage system — (1) keep score=1.0 docs, (2) return the one with lowest embedding distance — achieves near-perfect retrieval on this dataset. This is the primary actionable finding.

Distance vs Score Analysis

These charts visualise the global relationship between LLM score and proximity to GT in embedding space, across all scored pairs.

ℹThe scatter shows clear vertical stripes — confirming score discretisation (only 10 values). GT docs (red) cluster near dist=0 as expected. The decile chart shows a meaningful decline in mean LLM score from Q1 (closest to GT) to Q10 (farthest), but the median is flat at 0.1 — most docs score the minimum regardless.

Failure Case Analysis

In 4 queries the LLM did not assign the maximum score to GT — it was outscored by at least one non-GT document. These are the true LLM failures.

query_idxgt_llm_scoregt_dist_to_gtn_docs_above_gtgt_rankmax_score_in_queryn_docs_at_maxn_docs_total
43.00.80.0000005.06.01.05.06000.0
87.00.60.00000027.028.01.026.06000.0
107.00.10.000000280.0281.01.03.04000.0
140.00.80.0000008.09.01.08.04000.0
⚠Query 107 is the worst failure: GT scored 0.1, ranked 281st out of 5 000+ docs. Queries 43 and 140 scored 0.8 (just below the top tier). These failures are consistent with edge cases where the GT document may be unusually short, technical, or differently worded from the query.

Conclusions

✓LLM as coarse filter (excellent) — At threshold 1.0, pool shrinks ~450× while retaining GT in 98% of queries. Precision in the retained set is 8.8% (1 GT among ~11 docs).
⚠LLM as precise ranker (limited) — Tie-corrected MRR = 0.257, not 0.98. The model cannot discriminate within the top-scoring tier.
★Hybrid pipeline (near-perfect) — LLM filter (score=1.0) → embedding-distance tiebreaker recovers GT in 100% of the top-tier queries (97.5% overall, accounting for 4 failures where GT never reaches score=1.0).
ℹFalse positives are semantically real — Non-GT docs scoring 1.0 have median dist_to_gt ≈ 0.3. They are genuine semantic neighbours of GT, not noise.
ℹNegative correlation confirmed across all methods — Spearman −0.17, Pearson −0.39, Kendall −0.13. Higher LLM score consistently predicts lower embedding distance to GT.

Extended Summary (raw)

EXTENDED ANALYSIS SUMMARY — CORRECTED INTERPRETATION
THE TIE PROBLEM (why base P@1=98% is misleading)
  Queries where GT reaches top score tier : 195 / 199
  Queries where GT is UNIQUELY top-scorer : 6 / 199
  Median # docs tied at GT's score        : 9
  Expected tier precision (1/n_tied)      : 0.172
  Interpretation: LLM correctly classes GT as 'max relevance'
  but puts ~11 other docs in the same class — it cannot pinpoint GT.
CORRECTED RETRIEVAL METRICS (tie-aware)
  MRR (optimistic, rank_min)   : 0.9815
  MRR (tie-corrected, rank_avg): 0.2571
  MRR (pessimistic, rank_max)  : 0.1695
QUERY DIFFICULTY
  EASY   (GT only top-scorer)  : 6 queries
  MEDIUM (GT + 1-9 others tied): 100 queries
  HARD   (GT + 10+ others tied): 89 queries
  FAILED (GT outscored by others): 4 queries
REVISED CONCLUSION
The LLM is excellent at binary relevance detection: it assigns score=1.0
to the GT document in 98% of queries (ROC-AUC=0.996). However, it cannot
discriminate at fine-grained level within the top tier — on average 9
documents share the top score per query. Tie-corrected MRR drops from
0.981 to 0.257, reflecting that LLM alone cannot replace the retriever —
it needs a secondary signal (e.g., dist_to_gt or cosine similarity) to
break ties within the top-scoring group. As a FILTER (keep score=1.0
docs, avg pool of ~11), the LLM is very effective. As a RANKER (pick
single top doc), it needs tie-breaking.
